properties.remark: A waterspout was reported by the public. Was thin, and appeared to look as a funnel cloud. Duration unknown.
A waterspout was observed by Higgs Beach. This waterspout was thin, and a light spray ring was visible. It lasted a few minutes.
A waterspout was observed south of Big Pine and while on the 7 mile bridge. Was thin and thought to be not fully formed. No spray ring visible. Duration unknown.
Weatherflow mesonet station Carysfort Reef Light recorded a thunderstorm wind gust of 34 knots or 39 mph at 310pm EDT April 8th.

A family of waterspouts were observed by the public in Niles Channel between Summerland Key and Big Pine Key. Waterspouts formed and collapsed over the course of the hour. Waterspouts varied between thick and thin funnels with observed spray rings.
A family of waterspouts were observed by the public north of Big Pine Key. Waterspouts appeared as full condensation funnels. Visibility of spray ring was not confirmed because of obstructions.
A family of waterspouts were reported by the public north of Bahia Honda State Park. Waterspouts were fully down, and too far away to observe a spray ring.
A family of waterspouts were reported by the public between Summerland and Big Pine Key. Waterspout had a developed condensation funnel and a spray ring.

Corrects previous funnel cloud report from 5 W Route 1/Blackwater Sound on Thursday, May 22nd at 740 PM EDT. The funnel cloud was confirmed to be a waterspout, and the LSR has been corrected to reflect this. Between 740 and 745 PM EDT, a member of the public reported a thunderstorm located approximately 5 miles northwest of Key Largo that produced a couple of funnel clouds while the storm was in the vicinity of Blackwater Sound. There were no spray rings associated with any of the funnels.
Mesonet station XCFL Carysfort Reef Light recorded a thunderstorm wind gust of 35 knots, or 40 miles per hour, at 905 PM EDT on Thursday, May 22nd.

A member of the public reported a waterspout to the south of the Seven Mile Bridge occurring at 944 AM EDT. The photo accompanying the report showed a mature waterspout with a condensation shell extending to the surface of the water, and a visible spray ring.

A waterspout was sighted by an NWS Employee just southeast of Bahia Honda State Park at around 1000 AM EDT. The base was not visible and the funnel sighted below the base of the low cumulus lasted only a few minutes.

Corrects previous marine tstm wind report from 5 SSE Islamorada. The Alligator Reef Weatherflow mesonet site recorded a 49 knot wind gust out ahead of a collapsing thunderstorm. The station recorded wind gusts greater than 40 knots for more than 15 minutes.
The Alligator Reef Weatherflow mesonet site recorded a 49 knot wind gust out ahead of a collapsing thunderstorm.
The Islamorada Substation Weatherflow mesonet measured a 34 knot wind gust during a recent thunderstorm.
The Carysfort Reef Weatherflow mesonet measured a 42 knot wind gust during a recent thunderstorm.
A west wind gust of 34 knots was measured at the National Ocean Service gauge at the Florida Keys National Marine Sanctuary Nancy Foster Center.
A wind gust of 34 knots was measured at the Sand Key Light C-MAN station.

A thin, vertical waterspout was reported via a social media video near Snipe Point around 4:30 PM EDT. The condensation cloud extended from the cloud base to the surface, but a spray ring was not visible. Movement was west-southwest around 10 mph. Duration was between 5 and 10 minutes.

A thin, slightly tilted waterspout was observed south of Key West around 9:15 AM EDT, reported via a photo on social media. The condensation funnel extended at least a third of the way from the cloud base to the surface, but view of the lower two-thirds to the horizon was obstructed by a building. Movement was WSW around 15 mph. It was observed for 4 minutes prior to losing visual contact.
An ENE wind gust of 35 knots, or 40 mph, was recorded by the KNQX ASOS at NAS Key West - Boca Chica.
An ENE wind gust of 39 knots, or 45 mph, was recorded by the KEYW ASOS at Key West International Airport.
An ENE wind gust of 47 knots, or 54 mph, was recorded by the XKYW WeatherFlow mesonet site at USCG Sector Key West.
The Sombrero Key C-MAN station recently gusted to 35 knots during a thunderstorm.

A waterspout was observed north of Ramrod Key around 3:57 PM EDT, reported by an off-duty NWS employee. The condensation funnel was well developed and extended at least a third of the way from cloud base to surface, but additional details were obscured by buildings and trees. It was observed for a few minutes before losing visual contact and dissipated after loss of visual contact.

A family of 3 or so waterspouts were observed south of Bahia Honda Key at around 12:14 PM EDT, called in by a member of the public. A spray ring was observed on one of the waterspouts with funnels seen in the cloud bases for the others. Exact duration is unknown as caller phoned in once the waterspouts were spotted. Movement was to the southwest at around 10 knots.
An east wind gust of 35 knots, or 40 mph, was recorded by the XASL WeatherFlow mesonet site at American Shoal Light.
The WeatherFlow mesonet station XHKB at Harbor Key Light recorded a thunderstorm wind gust of 34 knots, or 39 miles per hour, at 420 PM EDT on Tuesday, August 5.
The Weatherflow mesonet station XHKB at Harbor Key Light recorded a marine thunderstorm wind gust of 39 knots, or 45 mph, at 805 PM EDT on Wednesday, August 6.
A NE wind gust of 35 knots, or 40 mph, was recorded by the XASL Weatherflow mesonet site at American Shoal Light.
A NE wind gust of 34 knots, or 39 mph, was recorded by the XASL Weatherflow mesonet site at American Shoal Light.
A NW wind gust of 36 knots, or 41 mph, was recorded at the SANF1 Sand Key Light C-MAN station.
A WNW wind gust of 34 knots, or 39 mph, was recorded by the XASL Weatherflow mesonet site at American Shoal Light.

A waterspout was reported approximately 4 miles offshore of MM74 at 7:24 PM by a member of the public. The condensation funnel was reported as reaching the surface. It was ongoing at the time of the report.

A mature, narrow waterspout with a visible spray ring was observed by broadcast media just south of Bow Channel Bridge, east of Upper Sugarloaf Key. It was tilted westward at about a 30 degree angle from the waters surface to the cloud base, moving WSW around 10 knots. The waterspout dissipated at 2:05 PM, with a duration of approximately 5 minutes.
A north wind gust of 40 knots, or 46 mph, was recorded by the XCFL WeatherFlow mesonet site at Carysfort Reef Light.
A north wind gust of 37 knots, or 42 mph, was recorded by the KYWF1 NOS Key West Tide Gauge.
A north wind gust of 34 knots, or 39 mph, was recorded by the KYWF1 NOS Key West Tide Gauge.
A wind gust of 40 knots, or 46 mph, was recorded by the CW0925 CWOP mesonet site on Cudjoe Key.

A skinny, rope-like, mature waterspout with a spray ring was observed off John Pennekamp State Park this morning and reported via social media. It was tilted at a 45 degree angle northeastward from the cloud base to the waters surface. Movement was to the NE around 10 knots. Duration was approximately 2 minutes before it dissipated.
A south southwest wind gust of 35 knots, or 40 mph, was recorded by the XCFL WeatherFlow mesonet station at Carysfort Reef Light.

Corrects previous waterspout report from 3 SSW Boca Chica. Several members of the public and NWS employees observed a family of 2-4 waterspouts about 2-3 miles to the south of Stock Island. The waterspouts were thin and upright, reaching the surface, although no spray ring could be observed. The waterspouts lasted for around 8 minutes.

Several members of the public and the NWS observed a waterspout about 2-3 miles to the south of Stock Island. The waterspout was thin and upright, reaching the surface, although no spray ring could be observed. The waterspout lasted for about 8 minutes.
Corrects previous marine tstm wind report from 2 E Key West for erroneous magnitude. An east wind gust of 38 knots, or 44 mph, was recorded by the KEYW ASOS at Key West International Airport.
A mature, moderately thick waterspout with a visible spray ring was observed by a member of the public just north of Boca Grande Key. Duration of the waterspout is unknown.

Corrects previous waterspout report from 14 W Key West. Several members of the public reported a pair of mature, moderately thick waterspouts with visible spray rings just north of Boca Grande Key. Duration of the waterspouts is unknown.
A north wind gust of 36 knots, or 41 mph, was recorded by the XKYW WeatherFlow mesonet site at USCG Sector Key West.
A north wind gust of 34 knots, or 39 mph, was recorded by the XALG WeatherFlow mesonet site at Alligator Reef Light.
An east wind gust of 35 knots, or 40 mph, was recorded by the Keys Energy Services mesonet site on James Street.
An east wind gust of 35 knots, or 40 mph, was recorded by the XKYW WeatherFlow mesonet site at USCG Sector Key West.
An ENE wind gust of 37 knots, or 43 mph, was recorded by the SANF1 C-MAN station at Sand Key Light.

Three to four waterspouts occurred south of Marathon on Saturday evening, and were reported via social media. All of the waterspouts were reported to have lasted approximately 10 to 15 minutes. Included in the report were two videos of two of the waterspouts. The first waterspout was a very skinny, mature waterspout with a visible spray ring, tilted at a 45 degree angle. The second waterspout appeared to be a little less mature with the condensation funnel reaching approximately halfway down to the surface from the base of the cloud, and with a very faint, almost indistinguishable spray ring. This waterspout had very minimal tilt to it. The parent cell was moving from west to east at approximately 10 to 15 knots.

A skinny, rope-like waterspout with an observed spray ring was reported approximately 3 miles offshore of Long Key bridge by a member of the public. The condensation funnel was reported as just touching the surface. It was ongoing at time of the report.
At 650 PM, a north northeast wind gust of 36 knots, or 41 mph, was recorded by the XCFL WeatherFlow mesonet station at Carysfort Reef Light.
A northwest wind gust of 37 knots, or 43 mph, was recorded by the KYWF1 NOS Key West Tide Gauge.

A skinny, straight up and down waterspout with an observed spray ring was reported approximately 4 miles north northwest of Nile Channel Bridge. The waterspout was observed for a few minutes before it tilted becoming more rope-like and eventually dissipating.
A northwest wind gust of 35 knots, or 40 mph, was recorded by the XCFL WeatherFlow mesonet station at Carysfort Reef Light.
A north northwest wind gust of 40 knots, or 46 mph, was recorded by the KYWF1 NOS Key West Tide Gauge.
A northwest wind gust of 38 knots, or 44 mph, associated with a thunderstorm over the water was recorded by the Harbor Key Bank Light WeatherFlow mesonet site XHKBr.

A skinny, straight vertical waterspout was reported approximately 3 miles north of the Boca Chica Bridge. The presence of a spray ring could not be determined due to trees. The waterspout was ongoing at the time of the report.

Florida Keys National Marine Sanctuary staff reported a waterspout with an associated spray ring NNW of Sand Key Light at 2:05 PM EDT. The slightly tilted condensation cloud was of medium width and reached three quarters of the way from the cloud base to the surface at its greatest extent. Movement was slowly southward. Duration was 15 minutes with it dissipating at 2:20 PM EDT.

At 12:09 PM EDT, a member of the public reported three waterspouts to the south of Sandspur Beach at Bahia Honda State Park. What was visible of each waterspout was very skinny. The first waterspout extended straight down, approximately one half to three quarters of the way down, from the base of the parent storm, but a spray ring was not visible. The first waterspout lasted approximately 10 minutes. The second water spout had a visible condensation funnel that extended approximately one quarter to one half of the way to the surface, but a spray ring was visible. The second waterspout lasted approximately a couple of minutes. The third waterspout from the same parent storm was short lived, and only the funnel was clearly visible on the video. The third waterspout last even less time than the second waterspout.
A northeast wind gust of 42 knots, or 48 mph, was recorded by the LONF1 C-MAN station at Long Key.
A thunderstorm produced a wind gust of 39 knots or 45 mph at approximately 11:20 pm EDT Thursday night as it moved southward through the area.
A thunderstorm produced a wind gust to 36 knots or 41 mph at approximately 1:19 am early Friday as the storm drifted southeastward.

A SE wind gust of 35 knots, or 40 mph, was recorded by the KEYW ASOS at the Key West International Airport. This wind gust was associated with a brief wake low that developed as a result of convection over the Florida Straits.

A thin, near vertical, mature waterspout was observed offshore from the public boat ramp on the Atlantic side of U.S. 1 at Mile Marker 11, and reported via social media. At its most mature stage, the condensation column, and the nearly transparent sheath, appear very close to the surface. However, trees/mangroves in the foreground block any visual of a spray ring. Considering the proximity to the surface, there was likely a visible spray ring. The waterspout lasted for more than a few minutes, but less than ten minutes.
A SE wind gust of 34 knots, or 39 mph, was recorded by the KMTH ASOS at the Marathon International Airport.

A skinny, tube-like mature waterspout was observed off of Smathers Beach in Key West this evening by both the public and an NWS Meteorologist. It was tilted at an approximately 45 degree angle from the cloud base, and the condensation funnel extended nearly 3/4 of the way down. The waterspout dissipated after 13 minutes.
